summary: On Friday, Mohammad-Bagher Ghalibaf, Speaker of Iran's Parliament, declared there will be no negotiations with the U.S. unless a ceasefire in Lebanon is established and Iran's "blocked assets" are released.

Tensions escalated after Vice President Vance dismissed Iran's claim that the two-week ceasefire included Lebanon as a "misunderstanding," while Ghalibaf argued the "workable basis on which to negotiate" was violated before talks began.
Amid the diplomatic standoff, Israel launched a wave of attacks on Lebanon Wednesday, killing over 300 people and injuring over 1,100 others, with nearly 1,900 deaths since March.

Vice President Vance, Steve Witkoff, and Jared Kushner are scheduled to hold talks with Iranian officials in Islamabad on Saturday, while Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu ordered his Cabinet to negotiate with Lebanese officials focusing on disarming Hezbollah.

Ghalibaf previously cited drone incursions and enrichment pressures as violations of Iran's 10-point proposal, suggesting these unresolved issues could stall the upcoming diplomatic process to transform the ceasefire into a permanent truce.
title: Source Icon

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the 3.3% annual jump as a politically driven crisis—linking it to "Trump’s war," using verbs like "soars"/"skyrocketed" and phrases such as "squeezing households" while spotlighting winners like asset managers—whereas right-leaning pieces portray the move as a concentrated energy blip—coining "warflation" but stressing it’s "limited to energy" and "in line with expectations," downplaying persistence despite gasoline jumping roughly 20–21%.
Center coverage emphasizes the precise BLS stats and Fed implications.
Dividing lines include blame attribution, distributional impacts, and core‑ inflation persistence; all agree the gas spike drove the report and complicates Fed rate cuts.
summary: On Friday, the Bureau of Labor Statistics reported consumer prices rose 0.9% in March, pushing annual inflation to 3.3%, driven by a 21% gasoline price spike.
Global energy prices surged after Iran's de facto blockade of the Strait of Hormuz began in late February, constraining crude oil flows and triggering the energy shock.
Excluding volatile food and energy components, 'core' inflation rose a modest 0.2% for the month and 2.6% from a year ago, indicating underlying price pressures remain contained.
Traders have dialed back expectations for Federal Reserve interest-rate cuts this year, while Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell noted policymakers remain attentive to risks amid the energy shock.

Economists warn that higher energy costs will likely ripple through energy-intensive industries like airlines and shipping in coming months, further eroding purchasing power for U.S. households already stretched by elevated costs.
title: US Inflation Rose to 3.3% in March, Driven by Rising Fuel Costs

bias: Left outlets frame the ruling as a vindication of press freedom, using terms like "violating," "restore access," "evade/circumvent" and emotive verbs such as "humiliated" or "hammered" to portray the Pentagon and Hegseth as suppressors; right outlets spotlight judicial enforcement but qualify it with labels like "Clinton judge," emphasize "orders" or "Full" remedies and invoke "Kafkaesque" or national-security trade‑offs to cast doubt.
Center coverage is more process-focused, noting a "scathing" opinion.
Dividing lines include the judge's appointment, the "mark of an autocracy" line, and whether officials' intent was evasive; all sides agree the court found the DoD violated its order.
summary: On Thursday, U.S. District Judge Paul Friedman ruled the Defense Department is violating his earlier order to restore reporter access, effectively blocking the agency's revised press policy issued last month.

Following Friedman's March ruling, the Pentagon implemented new restrictions including mandatory escorts for journalists and closed the Correspondents Corridor, claiming these measures complied with the court's order.

Friedman wrote the department "cannot simply reinstate an unlawful policy under the guise of taking new action," while striking down its classification of press access as a "privilege" rather than a "right."
Friedman emphasized the case concerns the Secretary of Defense's efforts to dictate information received by the public, describing the department's actions as an attempt to "control the message.
title: Federal judge finds Pentagon is violating court order to restore access to reporters

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the surprise White House address as politically fraught—calling it "bizarre" or "desperate," spotlighting resurfaced emails with Maxwell and questioning timing as a preemptive move—while right-leaning pieces portray the remarks as vindicating, stressing denials with words like "lies" and "stunning" and praising her call for hearings.
A pivotal dividing line is attention to the email/Maxwell correspondence and internal White House confusion ; center outlets use "rare"/"extraordinary" neutrality.
Both sides agree she denied ties, urged hearings, and reignited the Epstein files , reflecting broader ideological divides over accountability versus defense.
summary: On Thursday, April 9, 2026, First Lady Melania Trump delivered a White House statement denying any friendship or relationship with Jeffrey Epstein and Ghislaine Maxwell, rejecting circulating claims as "lies."

The unannounced address followed federal authorities releasing millions of pages of documents under the Epstein Files Transparency Act, which included a 2002 email exchange with Maxwell that Trump dismissed as "casual correspondence."

Trump denied that Epstein introduced her to President Donald Trump, stating she met her husband by chance at a New York City party in 1998 and only attended the same parties occasionally due to overlapping social circles.
During her remarks, Trump called on Congress to hold public hearings for survivors to testify under oath, a request that Rep. Robert Garcia of the House Oversight Committee immediately supported.
Garcia urged Chairman James Comer to schedule hearings immediately, creating pressure on the committee to secure survivors' accounts for the congressional record regarding the Epstein files.
title: Melania Trump blasts 'lies' linking her to Epstein and calls on Congress to hold hearing for victims

bias: Left-Leaning outlets portray Israeli military actions as "attacks" that "imperil" diplomacy, emphasizing "U.S. pressure" as the primary catalyst.
Conversely, right-leaning sources frame the development as Netanyahu "ordering" a "purge" of Hezbollah following Lebanese "requests.
While the left uses language like "pummels" to highlight 300 casualties as a threat to peace, the right emphasizes "restoring security" through "talks under fire.
Center-Leaning reports characterize the shift as a "concession" or "strategic failure.

Both perspectives acknowledge Netanyahu authorized negotiations for Hezbollah’s disarmament, reflecting a broader ideological divide between prioritizing immediate humanitarian de-escalation and achieving peace through military strength.
summary: On Thursday, Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu instructed his cabinet to begin direct negotiations with Lebanon 'as soon as possible,' focusing on disarming Hezbollah and establishing peaceful relations between the two countries.

U.S. envoy Steve Witkoff pressed Netanyahu to 'calm down' strikes in Lebanon and open negotiations, following Lebanon's repeated requests and earlier calls from Lebanese President Joseph Aoun for a diplomatic path.

Negotiations will focus on disarming Hezbollah and establishing peace, yet Israeli strikes on Wednesday killed at least 254 people according to Lebanese Civil Defense, underscoring the humanitarian toll complicating diplomatic efforts.

The U.S. and Israel deny the ceasefire covers Lebanon, while Iran and Pakistan claim it explicitly includes the country, with Tehran warning that ongoing strikes constitute a 'blatant violation' of the agreement.

Vice President JD Vance leads a U.S. delegation to Islamabad this weekend for peace talks aimed at ending the war, as negotiations face significant hurdles amid regional uncertainty over ceasefire terms.
title: Netanyahu says Israel will begin 'direct negotiations' with Lebanon aimed at disarming Hezbollah

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames Artemis II as a "resounding success" while foregrounding human risk—calling reentry "very dangerous" and amplifying human‑interest and political context—whereas right-leaning pieces cast the outcome as "perfect"/"flawless," use dramatic verbs like "extracted" and "fiery return," and link the mission to a "great power struggle" with China, touting heat‑shield performance and four astronauts’ safe splashdown after a ~10‑day flight as proof of leadership; center outlets are more technical, comparing reentry to Apollo, and all agree the crew returned safely and the mission was historic, reflecting ideological splits over risk, national pride, and geopolitics.
summary: On Friday, April 10, 2026, Commander Reid Wiseman, Pilot Victor Glover, Mission Specialist Christina Koch, and Canadian Space Agency astronaut Jeremy Hansen splashed down in the Pacific Ocean off San Diego, ending Artemis II's historic 10-day lunar flyby.
Testing the Orion spacecraft and Space Launch System architecture, the mission set a human distance record of 252,756 miles from Earth, surpassing the Apollo 13 milestone by over 4,000 miles.

Enduring temperatures of nearly 5,000 degrees Fahrenheit during reentry, the capsule deployed 11 parachutes to slow from 300 mph to 20 mph for splashdown, while the crew captured more than 7,000 images and named a lunar crater 'Carroll' to honor Wiseman's late wife.

U.S. Navy recovery teams aboard the USS John P. Murtha retrieved all four astronauts, whom NASA confirmed were 'feeling great, happy to be home, and ready to be extracted' following medical assessments.

With Artemis II complete, NASA targets Artemis III for 2027 to test integrated operations with commercially built landers in low Earth orbit, establishing a long-term lunar presence as a stepping stone toward Mars.
title: Artemis II successfully splashes down, completing historic lunar mission

bias: Left coverage frames the story as a rebuke to hawkish rhetoric—saying U.S. intel "shot down" the claim that Iran was "functionally destroyed" and linking the finding to a "controversial ceasefire"—while right pieces amplify threat with "buried but not destroyed," "missile cities" and numeric alarms like "over 1,000" to stress imminent danger.
Rhetorically the left uses corrective, skeptical diction; the right uses alarmist, militarized language and urgency.
The center hedges with caveats.
All sides, however, accept the intel that Iran retains thousands and worry a ceasefire could enable rearmament, reflecting divides over security and accountability.
summary: The Wall Street Journal reported on Friday that United States intelligence assessments found Iran retains thousands of short- and medium-range ballistic missiles and could retrieve launchers from underground storage sites despite losing roughly half its stockpile.

Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth claimed on Wednesday the United States achieved a "decisive military victory against Iran," asserting Iran's ballistic missile program was "functionally destroyed" and that leadership had "begged" for the ceasefire.

Joint Chiefs Chairman Gen. Dan Caine reported Iran lost 90% of weapon factories and 80% of air defenses; however, United States officials warned that many remaining missile launchers are inoperable yet capable of reactivation.

American officials expressed concern that Iran may use the current ceasefire to rebuild its arsenal, threatening the Strait of Hormuz, through which 20 percent of global oil traffic passes and where United States forces operate.

Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth stated the Strait of Hormuz is "open" and the United States military will be "hanging around" Iran to ensure compliance, though intelligence reports suggest the ceasefire's security gains may prove reversible.
title: Iran Has Thousands of Missiles and Could Retrieve Launchers, U.S. Intelligence Finds

summary: U.S. Vice President JD Vance arrived in Islamabad leading a delegation to negotiate a durable ceasefire with Iran and reduce hostilities in Lebanon.
The Iranian delegation, led by parliamentary speaker Mohammad Bagher Qalibaf, arrived earlier in Islamabad, with both sides preparing for talks at a heavily secured location.
Pakistan is mediating the negotiations, with involvement from its Foreign Minister Ishaq Dar, aiming for a permanent ceasefire.
The discussions occur amid ongoing conflicts including Israeli-Lebanese clashes and Iran's closure of the Strait of Hormuz, which has disrupted global energy routes and increased oil prices.
title: Iranian delegation arrives in Islamabad for US talks

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames ICE’s action with measured verbs and skeptical distancing—noting "birth tourism schemes" in quotes and de-emphasizing links to the president—while right-leaning pieces amplify threat and enforcement, using charged terms like "massive," "crackdown," "uncover," and alleging networks that "lie on visa applications" and threaten "birthright citizenship.
Center outlets strike a middle tone, calling it a "decisive move" and foregrounding a Reuters-obtained email as sourcing.

The pivotal divides are whether the initiative is framed as organized criminality versus political theater and whether the Reuters email and presidential motive are emphasized; all agree ICE launched an effort targeting purported birth-tourism networks.
summary: On Thursday, U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement ordered agents nationwide to prioritize a new Birth Tourism Initiative targeting organized networks that facilitate travel for foreign nationals to give birth in the United States.
President Donald Trump's administration has used birth tourism as a rationale for restricting birthright citizenship after taking office in January 2025 to reduce overall immigration.

White House spokeswoman Anna Kelly stated the practice 'poses a tremendous cost to taxpayers and threatens our national security.' The Center for Immigration Studies estimated between 20,000 and 25,000 mothers visited the United States for birth tourism annually between 2016 and 2017.

Federal agents will leverage 2020 regulations prohibiting temporary tourist visas for citizenship purposes, targeting what the agency described as 'fraud, financial crimes, and organized facilitation networks that exploit lawful immigration processes.'

In 2019, federal prosecutors charged more than a dozen people operating 'birth houses' in Southern California catering to wealthy women from China; operator Chao 'Edwin' Chen was sentenced to three years in prison in 2020.
title: ICE launches new effort to uncover US ‘birth tourism schemes’
topic_full: Immigration and Customs Enforcement · United States

bias: Left-Leaning pieces portray the proposal as a pro-worker milestone—using "historic" and highlighting impacts on "nursing home workers"—and cast the administration’s action with forceful verbs like "temporarily blocks" and "faces another delay," conveying setback and moral outrage; right-leaning coverage, by contrast, uses leaner, procedural language—emphasizing a "reset to day one," the "90‑day" review and "delay"—framing oversight rather than obstruction and de-emphasizing celebratory labels; the pivotal split is whether the plan is an historic win or simply a rule needing review; center coverage adds date/context; all agree the pause/reset delays implementation for thousands of workers.
summary: On Wednesday, the Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services reset its 90-day review clock on day 89, halting Minnesota 's nursing home wage floor approval process.
Federal approval is mandatory because the wage floor stipulates that CMS will provide $18 million to Minnesota's Medicaid program to help nursing homes cover salary increases.
The nursing home industry recently filed a lawsuit claiming the Board "inflicts irreparable harm" on providers, while state law requires employees earn at least $19 hourly.
Leah Solo, executive director of the Nursing Home Workforce Standards Board, remains unaware what information CMS requested, as the Department did not convey the letter's contents.
A federal court hearing is scheduled next month regarding the industry's request for an injunction against the Board, as legal challenges intensify the wage floor stalemate.
title: Trump Administration Temporarily Blocks Minnesota’s Historic Wage Floor for Nursing Home Workers

bias: Left-Leaning coverage emphasizes the trade‑off as a win for students, using phrases like "helped expand" and "tuition‑free" to spotlight benefits; right-leaning pieces frame the move as a corrective reallocation from a "multimillion‑dollar DEI initiative," employing terse verbs like "shifts funding" to imply prudence.
The center focuses on precise figures and de-emphasizes judgment.

Both sides nonetheless agree the shift more than doubled Go Blue aid—near $56 million to over 6,300 students—while debates over the DEI "shutdown," missing budget details, and absent stakeholder reactions drive the sharpest partisan divides rooted in resource‑allocation and cultural priorities.
summary: The University of Michigan more than doubled financial aid this year through its Go Blue Guarantee, awarding an estimated $55.97 million to 6,387 students across Ann Arbor, Dearborn and Flint campuses one year after shutting down its multimillion-dollar DEI initiative.

U-M Board Chair Mark Bernstein said the millions invested in the DEI initiative "was refocused on the students, and the expansion of the Go Blue Guarantee is clearly a result of that refocus." Officials shifted the majority of DEI funding into student support programs.

Nearly 70% more students participated this year—2,601 additional enrollees compared to 3,786 students awarded $26.25 million in 2025. Since U-M launched the program in 2018 as part of the DEI initiative, it has provided $223.8 million to 25,796 students.

Beyond tuition, the university increased access to mental health services through its "Let's Talk" program and expanded the Blavin Scholars Program, which supports undergraduates from foster care or without parental support. It continues funding the Trotter Multicultural Center and Spectrum Center.

Coverage for teacher preparation, nursing and social work students now includes housing costs, while the Wolverine Pathways program continues. U-M also provides 24/7 AI tutors and personal AI assistants to improve academic success across campus.
title: University of Michigan Shifts Funding From DEI Into Its Free Tuition Program, Go Blue Guarantee

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the story as systemic failure—humanizing the subject with "man" and words like "forgotten" and "nearly half a century" to spotlight 47–48 years on death row and claims he had no lawyer for "nearly four decades"—portraying the overturn as corrective and moral.
Right-Leaning pieces adopt a legalistic, restrained tone, using "inmate" and "overturned" to emphasize procedure and the original conviction while de-emphasizing attorney neglect.
Center coverage foregrounds competency and legal rationale rather than moral indictment.
Both sides agree the sentence was overturned and note disability, revealing a split between reformist empathy and law‑and‑order formalism.
summary: Texas' highest criminal court overturned the death sentence of Clarence Curtis Jordan, 70, who was on death row for 48 years for murdering a Houston grocer in 1978.
Jordan, who is intellectually disabled, was found incompetent in subsequent years and could not be executed, but spent nearly four decades without an attorney advocating for him.
After new legal advocacy, the Texas Court of Criminal Appeals vacated Jordan's death sentence and sent the case back to Harris County for a new punishment proceeding.
title: Texas’ highest criminal court overturns sentence of inmate who has been on death row for 48 years

bias: Left-Leaning coverage emphasizes human context and caution—using terms like "allegedly" and highlighting messages to a partner and roommate—framing the story around the suspect’s state of mind; center pieces foreground the affidavit and chronology, calling the note a "chilling message" and listing seized items; right-leaning reports deploy sensational tactics—all caps "REVEALED", words like "confesses", "assassination", and naming the "lover"—to portray a politically targeted plot.

Key dividing lines are use of "assassination", publishing the partner’s name, and whether warrant inventories appear; all sides cite the affidavit, revealing ideological priorities about motive versus spectacle.
summary: Recently unsealed warrants from the State Bureau investigation reveal new evidence against Tyler Robinson , accused of killing Charlie Kirk, a 31-year-old political activist, at Utah Valley University on September 10, 2025.
Robinson faces capital murder charges and a potential death sentence for Kirk's death during the activist's Turning Point USA tour at the university campus.
DNA evidence including fingerprints and a smeared palm print from the Losee Center building was recovered, along with cellphone data and a Google Maps route leading toward the UVU campus.
Warrants detail a handwritten letter Robinson left for his partner, Lance Twiggs, instructing him to "drop what you are doing" and "look under my keyboard."
Robinson is scheduled to appear before District Court on April 17, where defense counsel has filed a motion to prevent cameras and microphones from the proceedings.
title: Alleged Charlie Kirk assassin Tyler Robinson's fingerprints, palm print found near rooftop: report

bias: Left-Leaning narratives frame the raid as an "escalating crackdown" on civil society, using pejoratives like "Silovists" to portray security forces as intrusive and mocking the "absurd" demand for a state "ideologist.
Right-Leaning outlets similarly highlight "government overreach" but focus on the suppression of "private businesses.
While center-leaning coverage de-emphasizes the "ideologist" requirement, it uniquely attributes the "repression" directly to the president.

All sides emphasize the detention of "52 employees" as the year's largest raid, revealing a shared condemnation of authoritarian tactics despite differing ideological focuses on professional versus economic freedom.
summary: On Thursday, Belarusian authorities detained 52 people at the Minsk offices of ZROBIM Architects, including founder Andrei Makouski, marking the country's largest single raid this year, according to the Viasna human rights center.
Authorities had demanded the private firm hire a full-time 'ideologist' to monitor its staff, and detainees faced suspicion of disloyalty, according to Viasna's account of events preceding the raid.

Lawyer Pavel Sapelka of Viasna told The Associated Press that authorities now arrest individuals and hack their devices before filing charges, while increasingly using 'extremism' designations to criminalize dissent with penalties of up to 10 years.
President Alexander Lukashenko has ruled the 9.5 million-person nation with an iron fist for more than three decades, and Viasna reports 913 political prisoners currently remain behind bars.
title: Belarus Detains More than 50 at Architectural Firm in Escalating Crackdown

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the event as a cosmopolitan, identity-focused development—using labels like "international mayor" and "honorary" to convey prestige and mild curiosity—while right-leaning pieces frame it with suspicion and ridicule, emphasizing verbs like "granted," phrases like "jetsetting" and even "certified weirdo" to suggest disloyalty or theatrics.
Rhetorically the left favors a bemused, celebratory tone; the right uses sarcasm, ad-hominem and sensational capitalization; the center emphasizes verification .
The left de-emphasized the spokesperson and legal/practical implications.
All report the decree granted citizenship at his request and note prior Albanian ties, reflecting a broader globalism-versus-national-loyalty divide.
summary: On Friday, Albanian President Bajram Begaj issued an official decree granting citizenship and a passport to former New York City Mayor Eric Adams upon his request.
Adams' affinity for Albania includes a previously unannounced four-day trip in 2025 where he met Prime Minister Edi Rama to discuss business and tourism opportunities.
Spokesperson Todd Shapiro confirmed the recognition, stating it "further strengthens the bond between New York and Albania," though Adams has not publicly confirmed obtaining the citizenship.
Adams received citizenship 100 days after leaving office, a period when his son competed in an Albanian "American Idol" contest and he sought to build international relationships.
title: Eric Adams, Former 'International Mayor' of NYC, Becomes an Honorary Albanian Citizen

bias: Left coverage frames the story as urgent ecological threat, using charged labels like "palm‑killing" and "highly invasive pest" and repetition to amplify damage, while right coverage is terse and event‑focused—highlighting a "suspected detection" near Kaunakakai Harbor and de‑emphasizing broader history—using cautious, localizing language.
Center pieces cast the sighting as "emblematic" of a decades‑long invasive‑ species struggle and stress "surveying" and pattern.
Pivotal divides are severity versus provisionality and local incident versus systemic context; all sides still report the detection, state surveys, and calls for residents to check palms.
summary: State officials are surveying Maui and Moloka'i following detections of the invasive coconut rhinoceros beetle near Kahului Airport and Kaunakakai Harbor. The suspected male beetle found Tuesday on Moloka'i marks the first time the pest has appeared on the island.

Invasive coconut rhinoceros beetles kill palm species by boring into their hearts and breed in compost. Residents successfully petitioned the Department of Agriculture and Biosecurity six months ago for strict import regulations to prevent this exact scenario.

Personnel from the Maui Invasive Species Committee and the Department are conducting surveys and deploying traps. Officials asked residents within a 2-mile radius of detection sites to check palms and report green waste piles.

Moloka'i Sen. Lynn DeCoite criticized the Department's communication, stating, "We can't help them if they're not communicating." Legislators threatened to pull over $20 million in funding unless the agency addresses staff vacancies.

Rep. Mahina Poepoe introduced House Bill 1929 this year to establish a resident advisory board and quarantine facility on Moloka'i. The bill advanced Wednesday after clearing the Senate Ways and Means Committee.
title: Palm-Killing Beetle Found On Molokaʻi For First Time, Rediscovered On Maui

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the story as a defense of the project—using language like "sues to stop" and "walking away" to cast the developer as preventing abandonment—while right-leaning pieces foreground contractual fallout, emphasizing "terminating its contracts" and that the developer "owes $300 million," portraying the dispute as industry failure and financial dispute; center outlets blend both and alone report the lawsuit’s claim of "inexcusably poor performance.

The pivotal divides are the $300 million allegation and contract-termination timing , with both sides nevertheless agreeing a lawsuit aims to force the manufacturer to stay, reflecting deeper pro-project vs. market-skeptic priorities.
summary: Vineyard Wind filed a lawsuit against GE Renewables after the latter tried to terminate contracts for turbine services and maintenance.
GE Vernova claims Vineyard Wind owes it $300 million, while Vineyard Wind says GE Renewables owes it $545 million due to a turbine blade collapse and delays.
The lawsuit states that allowing GE Renewables to back out would cause irreparable harm, as it is the only company able to perform the remaining work.
title: Developer of Massachusetts offshore wind farm sues to stop turbine manufacturer from walking away

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the case as victim-centered and procedural—highlighting the "American woman" who "vanished," the husband's "fragile state," and that police are "likely considering murder charge"—while right-leaning pieces foreground law-and-order with terms like "behind bars" and "arrested," stressing detention and public‑safety.
Center outlets amplify family claims of being "physically and emotionally abused" and an ex‑FBI agent who "picked holes" in the husband's story—angles de-emphasized by the right.
All sides report she is missing, he is detained, and police are weighing charges, revealing deeper punitive versus humanitarian priorities.
summary: The Royal Bahamas Police Force extended Brian Hooker's detention through Monday evening following his arrest in connection with his wife Lynette Hooker's disappearance, his attorney said Friday.
Brian Hooker told investigators his 55-year-old wife fell from an 8-foot dinghy near Elbow Cay on Saturday, with strong currents subsequently carrying her away before he could reach her.
Lynette Hooker's daughter, Aylesworth, questioned the account, asking, "Why wouldn't he drop anchor and look for her?" after a jarring notification call that left her skeptical.
Assistant Commissioner Advardo Dames of the Royal Bahamas Police Force cited "probable cause" for the arrest, while the search shifted to a recovery mission on Tuesday.

Terrel Butler, representing Brian Hooker, stated he "categorically and unequivocally denies any wrongdoing," noting his client's "intense frustration" over being unable to continue the search for his wife.
title: Husband of American woman missing in Bahamas will remain in police custody after being questioned, attorney says

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the episode as a journalistic-ethics issue—emphasizing reinvestigating prior coverage and newsroom oversight—while right-leaning pieces cast it as a "bombshell" or "scandal," using charged words like "sidelined," "cozy pics," and "grilled" to magnify reputational and familial fallout.
Rhetorically the left adopts accountability-focused language , the right prefers sensational, adversarial markers to erode media standing.
Center outlets foreground the private-investigator theory and personnel actions; the PI angle is de-emphasized by both sides and the left de-emphasized personnel-action phrasing.
Both agree photos circulated widely and major newsrooms are reviewing the matter, revealing deeper divides over media accountability versus media skepticism.
summary: The Athletic launched an internal investigation into NFL reporter Dianna Russini on Tuesday after photographs surfaced showing her with New England Patriots coach Mike Vrabel at Ambiente Sedona resort in Arizona.
Page Six published the images on March 28 after an anonymous tipster attempted to sell them to TMZ for a 'four-figure' sum, though Russini claimed the photos lacked context.

Editors are now pressing for evidence supporting Russini's assertion that a larger group of six people was present during the interactions, while The Athletic executive editor Steven Ginsberg initially defended her as a 'premier journalist.'
Sources told Fox News Digital that Russini is not currently reporting for The Athletic while her coverage remains under review, expected to take time as new details raise additional concerns.

Questions about newsroom norms regarding reporter-source interactions have intensified this week, with The New York Times ordering its own internal scrutiny as the probe aims to address broader concerns about journalistic integrity.
title: NYT and The Athletic Investigate Dianna Russini After Photos With Patriots Coach Surface

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the FY27, $21.2B plan as a "balanced budget" that protects social programs—citing "child care," "Medicaid," avoidance of tax hikes and handling a $1.1 billion gap through "strategic cuts"—emphasizing resident protections and pragmatic tradeoffs; right-leaning coverage foregrounds the plan’s size , stresses "cut spending," warns of reductions to programs and uses combative language like "spark/erupt into battles" and a "growing willingness to interject themselves into local affairs," signaling fiscal retrenchment and oversight concerns; centrist pieces lean into "boosts" to schools/infrastructure.
Agreement: education , health/public-safety priorities and impending political fights.
summary: On Friday, Mayor Muriel Bowser unveiled her final $21.2 billion FY27 "Grow DC" budget, prioritizing education and public safety while maintaining core city services.
Facing a $1.1 billion revenue shortfall, the budget addresses slower revenue growth and federal workforce reductions from the Trump administration's government efficiency efforts.
Bowser's plan eliminates the $60 million Pay Equity Fund and scales back private-sector paid leave by $95 million, while increasing per-student school funding to $15,455.
The D.C. Council begins a 70-day review to modify and pass the final version; Bowser assured members the city is "not broke," citing $2 billion in reserves.
This proposal marks Mayor Muriel Bowser's final budget presentation before she steps down later this year, having served since 2015 and announced she will not seek reelection.
title: Washington Mayor Muriel Bowser Unveils Her Last Budget, Proposing to Cut Spending
topic_full: Washington DC Politics · District of Columbia

bias: Left-Leaning coverage emphasizes traveler reassurance and the lasers' border‑control purpose, framing them as tools to "combat cartel drones" and repeating the FAA 's line that "proper use" is "safe"; right‑leaning coverage foregrounds the FAA‑DoD agreement, uses "deploying" language and reassuring phrases like "aren’t expected" to downplay closures and justify the measure.
The left adopts a calming, public‑safety tone while the right favors duty‑focused, pro‑security rhetoric; the center highlights technical testing that both sides de‑emphasize.
Both sides agree on the FAA finding and planned deployment, revealing a regulatory‑versus‑security split.
summary: The Federal Aviation Administration and the Pentagon agreed Friday that counter-drone lasers along the southern border are safe, ensuring additional airspace closures are not expected.
Earlier this year, the FAA shut down parts of Texas airspace twice after counter-drone lasers were deployed without proper coordination, causing travel disruptions near Paso.

A demonstration conducted last month at the White Sands Missile Range in New Mexico convinced the FAA that counter-drone lasers can be used safely. The FAA and Defense Department signed an agreement outlining safety precautions.
FAA Administrator Bryan Bedford said, "We will continue working with our interagency partners to ensure the National Airspace System remains safe while addressing emerging drone threats."
The Trump administration has worked to halt incursions by Mexican cartel drones along the southern border, where the Defense Department recently shot down a "seemingly threatening" drone.
title: FAA says proper use of anti-drone lasers that prompted Texas airspace closures is safe for flights

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the story as state culpability, using terms like "wrongful death" and "died in U.S. custody" to highlight government responsibility, while right-leaning pieces stress medical background—"chronic heart problems," "sickle cell anemia," "flu-like symptoms"—to question custody's role.
Centrist reports focus on alleged "medical neglect" and concrete details such as an eight-day detention.
Pivotal divides are alleged neglect versus preexisting conditions and the detention timeline.
All sides report the lawsuit, the child’s identity, and that she died in custody, underscoring broader ideological splits over accountability and immigration policy.
summary: The Honduran family of Anadith Danay Reyes Alvarez, an 8-year-old who died in U.S. custody in 2023, sued the federal government on Friday.

While detained for eight days in Donna and Harlingen, Texas, Anadith developed a fever of 104.9 F, nausea, and breathing difficulties; she was not taken to a hospital until her condition became critical.
An internal CPB investigation found medical personnel failed to review documents the mother provided describing the girl's sensitive chronic heart condition and sickle cell anemia.
Rossel Reyes Martinez, the girl's father, said Thursday the family filed suit "to ensure that no family has to endure the same pain we have endured." The lawsuit seeks damages for their loss.
This wrongful death lawsuit follows a tort claim filed against the government last year that was denied in October; the Department of Homeland Security has not yet responded to comment requests.
title: Family Sues US over 8-Year-Old's Death in Custody After Crossing the Border

bias: Left-Leaning pieces foreground victim-centered, sympathetic language—using terms like "survivors" and labels such as "convicted pedophile"—and even highlight a Fox News segment and a Sandra Smith clip to amplify Melania’s call; right-leaning coverage is briefer and administrative, framing Comer’s action as a response with phrasing like "responds to Melania Trump ’s call" and emphasizing agreement over context.

Center outlets use neutral descriptors and uniquely note the DOJ’s release of millions of files, which left and right de-emphasize; both sides nonetheless frame hearings as appropriate, exposing ideological splits over tone, emphasis, and accountability.
summary: On Friday, House Oversight Committee Chairman James Comer confirmed plans to hold public hearings for Jeffrey Epstein victims, following First Lady Melania Trump's call for such testimony on Thursday.

Melania Trump stated Thursday that victims should tell their stories under oath to uncover the truth, thrusting the Epstein matter back into the spotlight after documents identified more than 1,200 victims since late 2025.

"We will have hearings," Comer told Fox News on Friday, noting the committee must complete depositions of "more high-profile men" while his attorneys have communicated with survivors' legal representatives for months.

In February, the Oversight Committee questioned former President Bill Clinton and former Secretary of State Hillary Clinton about their Epstein dealings; both denied involvement, as has President Donald Trump.
While some survivors are willing to testify, others are not—a reality Comer said he understands as the committee processes evidence from millions of files released regarding Epstein's operations.
title: Comer responds to Melania Trump: ‘We will have hearings’ with Epstein victims

bias: Left-Leaning coverage emphasizes party accountability and rapid repudiation, framing actions with verbs like "withdraw," "demand," and foregrounding "sexual assault allegations" and calls to "drop out"; right-leaning pieces amplify scandal and moral condemnation, using charged terms such as "slam," "bombshell," "Indefensible" and narratives of a campaign "imploding"—sometimes with celebratory taunts like "couldn't happen to a better guy.
Centers stress verification—using "unverified" and "reeling"—and note details like a fundraising cutoff .
Both agree on the allegations, denials and rapid withdrawals, reflecting deeper ideological splits over accountability versus partisan scandal.
summary: On Friday, Rep. Eric Swalwell 's California governor campaign faltered after CNN reported allegations from four women detailing sexual misconduct, including unwanted touching and assault; Swalwell strongly denies the claims.

Following the report, campaign chair Rep. Jimmy Gomez resigned, stating he could not "in good conscience" remain involved, while longtime allies California Sen. Adam Schiff and Arizona Sen. Ruben Gallego withdrew their endorsements.

Former House Speaker Nancy Pelosi reportedly urged Swalwell to end his bid, while House Democratic leader Hakeem Jeffries called for a thorough investigation and rival Tom Steyer demanded he "resign from Congress and drop out."
Defending his record, Swalwell maintains the allegations are false, citing his "nearly 20 years" of public service, and stated he would "defend myself with the facts" and pursue legal action.

The controversy reshapes the race for California governor, threatening Swalwell's standing in a crowded primary featuring a half-dozen prominent Democrats and two leading Republicans with ballots due in June.
title: Democrats withdraw endorsements of Eric Swalwell and demand he end bid for California governor

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the story as humanitarian and managerial failure—using phrases like "cash crunch," "tiny fraction," and "shattered Palestinian enclave" to stress that a $17 billion pledge has produced only minimal payments and that three donors have paid—while right-leaning coverage foregrounds blunt operational failure and political blowback, deploying "'No money'" and dramatic wording such as "plunged the region into war," plus photos and attendee lists to emphasize reputational risk.
Center pieces are terser and de-emphasized; all agree the shortfall has stalled the Gaza plan, reflecting deeper ideological and geopolitical priorities.
summary: President Donald Trump 's Board of Peace halted its Gaza governance plan on Saturday, April 11, citing severe funding shortages. The board secured only a fraction of the $17 billion pledged, preventing the transition from Hamas.

The Iran war "has affected everything," exacerbating previous funding difficulties, the source said. Out of ten countries pledging support, only the United Arab Emirates, Morocco, and the U.S. have contributed, leaving total funds under $1 billion.

Led by Ali Shaath, the U.S.-backed National Committee for the Administration of Gaza remains unable to enter the territory. Security concerns and lack of financial resources prevent the committee from assuming control of Gaza's ministries or police.

Disarmament negotiations remain deadlocked, as Hamas refuses to lay down arms without guarantees of an Israeli withdrawal. Israeli troops maintain control over more than half of Gaza despite the ceasefire.

Global institutions estimate Gaza's rehabilitation will cost around $70 billion, as four-fifths of buildings were destroyed. This substantial financial gap, alongside regional instability, complicates long-term prospects for peace and reconstruction.
title: Trump's Peace Board Faces Cash Crunch, Stalling Gaza Plan, Sources Say
Houthis Confirm Coordination With Iran, Hezbollah In Several Attack Waves On Israel

bias: Left-Leaning coverage emphasizes the humanitarian toll—using words like "hundreds" and highlighting civilian deaths, regional escalation and Trump's "scaramocce" as minimization—framing Israel as the aggressor; right-leaning pieces stress military force and justification, deploying charged terms like "devastates" and "brutal raids," foregrounding concrete figures and diplomatic/security incidents to portray decisive action and fallout, while center coverage stresses the strikes' "unprecedented" scale and reports they occurred "without warning.
Key dividing lines are warnings to civilians, diplomatic incidents, and risk to a US–Iran truce; all agree strikes occurred, casualties were high, and the IDF reported ~100 targets.
summary: On Thursday, Israeli forces conducted over 100 airstrikes in roughly 10 minutes across Lebanon, despite a ceasefire announced between the United States and Iran hours earlier.
Renewed fighting between Israel and Hezbollah has caused unprecedented displacement, with some 1.2 million people uprooted from their homes throughout the region.
The Director General of the Red Cross confirmed more than 300 casualties following over 40 strikes in Beirut, while the WHO verified 106 attacks on healthcare services to date.
UNFPA estimates 13,500 pregnant women are among the displaced, with 200 expected to give birth in the next 30 days while "bearing an unprecedented and intolerable burden," Laila Baker said.
The Secretary-General's Personal Envoy for the Middle East Conflict arrived in Iran on Wednesday to support peace efforts, as the UN continues pushing for talks toward a permanent ceasefire.
title: Lebanon: Hundreds Killed and Wounded in New Israeli Attacks

bias: Left coverage frames the Islamabad talks through mistrust and U.S. belligerence—using words like "threatens" and "world waits" to show Trump-era warnings complicating diplomacy—while right coverage portrays Iran as obstructionist, stressing "preconditions," images like "blood-stained bags," and calls to "shore up" a "shaky cease-fire," casting U.S. envoys as stabilizers and Iran as theatrical.

Minab tributes and the delegation’s cross-sector makeup are pivotal dividing lines that provoke divergent tones; centrist reports de-emphasize partisanship, calling the talks "high-stakes" and procedural.
Both sides agree the goal is preserving a fragile ceasefire, revealing deeper security-versus-accountability divides.
summary: U.S. Vice President JD Vance arrived in Islamabad leading a delegation to negotiate a durable ceasefire with Iran and reduce hostilities in Lebanon.
The Iranian delegation, led by parliamentary speaker Mohammad Bagher Qalibaf, arrived earlier in Islamabad, with both sides preparing for talks at a heavily secured location.
Pakistan is mediating the negotiations, with involvement from its Foreign Minister Ishaq Dar, aiming for a permanent ceasefire.
The discussions occur amid ongoing conflicts including Israeli-Lebanese clashes and Iran's closure of the Strait of Hormuz, which has disrupted global energy routes and increased oil prices.
title: Iranian delegation arrives in Islamabad for US talks

bias: Left coverage is de‑emphasized in this sample, so the clearest split is between center and right: the center frames the story as an environmental‑justice narrative—spotlighting Barbara Johnson, a "mostly Black neighborhood" and emotive terms like "derailed" and "dirtiest" to foreground community harms—while the right foregrounds energy and policy mechanics, stressing increased demand from "data centers," "reviving coal‑fired power plants" and "reversing environmental regulations," with urgent language such as "the future of clean air hangs in the balance.
A pivotal divide is the causal link from data‑center growth to coal revival ; both sides, however, portray activists worried about health impacts.
summary: In February, President Donald Trump 's administration scrapped federal soot standards to support surging electricity demand from artificial intelligence data centers, reversing 2024 regulations that would have forced power plants to slash emissions.

St. Louis residents breathe "good" air during only one-third of the year, ranking 475th in air quality among 501 U.S. metro areas, while the Labadie Energy Center emits soot at two to three times the rate of other coal plants.

Soot pollution from power plants kills African Americans at a rate 25% higher than the national average, driving an economic burden of up to $5.5 billion annually, with about $820 million borne by St. Louis area residents.

Darnell Tingle, director of United Congregations of Metro-East, called the region a "sacrifice zone," while Ameren spokesperson Craig Giesman stated the utility remains focused on "operating responsibly" as data center demand outpaces cleaner power rollout.

The U.S. Department of Energy estimates artificial intelligence and data center growth will create 50 gigawatts of new electricity demand by 2030, a nearly 4% increase, forcing utilities to keep aging coal plants running.
title: How the AI boom derailed clean‑air efforts in one of America's most polluted cities

bias: Left-Leaning coverage foregrounds survivors’ voices and institutional accountability—using terms like "victim‑blaming," "weird presser," and urging Melania to "testify" under oath—while right-leaning pieces defend the first lady and delegitimize critics by calling the response "bizarre" and de‑emphasizing survivor details and the 15 signatories highlighted by center reporting.
Rhetorically, the left uses accusatory, morally charged language , the right adopts dismissive framing to shift focus; center stresses scope without overt moralizing.
Both report the denial, hearings call, and survivors’ criticism, reflecting wider accountability versus reputational-defense divides.
summary: On Thursday, First Lady Melania Trump denied any relationship with Jeffrey Epstein or accomplice Ghislaine Maxwell, calling on Congress to provide survivors with a public, sworn hearing.
A coalition of Epstein survivors and family members rejected the First Lady's remarks in a joint letter Thursday, labeling them "a deflection of responsibility, not justice."
Survivor Danielle Bensky warned that testifying without safety precautions could endanger victims, stating the proposal "shifts the burden onto survivors under politicized conditions."

Contradicting her denial, unredacted emails released in February show Melania Trump had frequent contact with Ghislaine Maxwell, challenging her account of no relationship with the criminal associate.
The public dispute increases pressure on lawmakers to determine how to pursue Epstein-related oversight while balancing survivor safety and independence from political influence.
title: Epstein survivors pan Melania Trump statement, say it ‘diverts attention’ from Bondi

bias: Left-Leaning coverage foregrounds victims and accountability—using phrases like "sexual misconduct," "sexually assaulting," the count "four women," and framing the campaign as "on the ropes"—portraying the story as an ethical crisis demanding resignations; right-leaning pieces deploy sensational tactics—"bombshell," "drops bombshell," "exposing himself," "sleazebag," "implodes"—to cast the allegations as partisan scandal and proof of Democratic hypocrisy, often flagging legal moves and prior controversies; center outlets de-emphasize rhetoric, focusing on core facts and denials, while all report the accusation, his denial, staff departures and campaign disruption, reflecting competing ideological priorities.
summary: On Friday, the San Francisco Chronicle published a report detailing sexual assault allegations against Representative Eric Swalwell , including claims from a former staffer who alleges he assaulted her twice while she was intoxicated.

These accusations follow weeks of circulating social media rumors regarding Swalwell's alleged inappropriate behavior toward young staffers, which his campaign previously dismissed as 'false, outrageous rumor' orchestrated by political opponents.

The former staffer alleged Swalwell pursued her shortly after she was hired in 2019 at age 21, initiating sexual contact during work travel and later forcing himself on her while she was severely intoxicated.

Major Democratic backers including Senator Adam Schiff and the California Teachers Association withdrew their endorsements Friday, while senior campaign staffer Courtni Pugh resigned amid mounting pressure for Swalwell to exit the race.

Swalwell categorically denied the allegations, labeling them politically motivated, while his legal team issued cease-and-desist letters to accusers, warning of potential defamation lawsuits regarding 'false statements.
title: Ex-Staffer Says That Rep. Eric Swalwell Sexually Assaulted Her

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames Engel through a visual, local lens—using a photo-retrospective and emphasizing his representation of the Bronx and Westchester—while right-leaning coverage de-emphasizes those local ties, deploys attention-grabbing tactics and casts him primarily as an antagonist, claiming he "spearheaded" Trump's first impeachment ; official House records do not support that leadership claim.
Center pieces foreground tenure .
The pivotal divide is whether Engel is presented as a community statesman or a partisan antagonist; all perspectives report his death, age , and Democratic affiliation.
summary: Former New York Congressman Eliot Engel died Friday, his family confirmed in a statement. He represented parts of the Bronx and Westchester County during 16 terms in the House of Representatives from 1989 through 2021.
Before his federal career, Engel served in the New York State Assembly for over 10 years. His family noted he died peacefully in "the borough that raised him: The Bronx."
As Chairman of the House Foreign Affairs Committee, Engel championed international security. U.S. Rep. Ritchie Torres called him "a giant of New York politics ."
Westchester County Executive Ken Jenkins stated Engel's legacy is "one of commitment, conviction and service." He was also a "fierce advocate" for Kosovo and the Albanian community.
Engel was first elected to Congress in 1988 but lost his seat in the 2020 Democratic primary to Jamaal Bowman. His family said he dedicated "over 44 years" to public service.
title: Eliot Engel, a 16-Term Bronx Liberal in Congress, Dies at 79

bias: Left-Leaning coverage condemns the remarks—labeling them a 'fascist threat' and framing 'loading up the ships' as hypocritical militarism—while right-leaning pieces celebrate decisive strength, using phrases like 'chilling ultimatum,' 'warships,' and 'best weapons ever' and pushing a 24‑hour deadline to amplify urgency.

Rhetorically, the left employs pejorative moralizing , the right deploys triumphant/alarmist language , and center outlets de-emphasize moralizing, neutrally quoting 'loading up the ships' and 'best ammunition.' Dividing triggers include a Truth Social post, the 24‑hour framing, and mention of a dispatched envoy; all agree the president threatened strikes if talks fail, reflecting deeper ideological splits over militarism versus deterrence.
summary: On Friday, President Donald Trump warned that U.S. naval vessels are prepared to conduct strikes if diplomacy with Iran fails, as reports indicate Iran is charging fees to tankers transiting the Hormuz Strait.

The White House intends to seek the release of Americans detained in Iran, though Iranian officials warned they will not attend crucial peace talks in Pakistan unless the ceasefire expands to Lebanon.

Consumer prices spiked in March as the Iran war sent energy costs soaring, according to the Bureau of Labor, while an association of airports warned Europe could face jet fuel shortages if ships are blocked from Hormuz.
Fears over rising energy prices pushed consumer confidence to a record low in April, and these soaring costs have moved the Federal Reserve further from its inflation target.

Donald Trump's shifting posture on Iran has raised questions about his stability, following earlier tensions between his administration and the Vatican, highlighting unpredictability in U.S. diplomatic signaling.
title: Trump says military is ‘loading up the ships’ if peace talks with Iran go south in Pakistan

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the 250-foot "triumphal arch" as ostentatious and self‑aggrandizing—using terms like "tacky," "pompous," and nicknames such as "Arc de Trump," spotlighting a veterans' lawsuit and claims it would alter sightlines near Arlington and the Lincoln Memorial—while right‑leaning coverage valorizes grandeur, using words like "iconic," emphasizing "gold/gilded" eagles, "One Nation Under God," and official praise that it will "honor our veterans" and bring "glory.

Rhetorically, the left adopts mocking, moral critique; the right uses reverent, celebratory diction; center reporting is procedural, noting the 12‑page filing and NEH allocations the sides de‑emphasize.
All agree on the renderings, height, symbols and site, reflecting deeper divides over national symbolism and legacy.
summary: On Friday, the Interior Department submitted renderings for a proposed 250-foot 'triumphal arch' at Memorial Circle to the Commission of Fine Arts for formal review.
President Donald Trump has promoted the project for months to commemorate America's 250th anniversary, saying he wants it to be the 'biggest one of all.'

Renderings by Harrison Design show a 250-foot structure topped by a winged Lady Liberty with four golden lions and 'One Nation Under God' inscribed in gold. The National Endowment for the Humanities has reserved $2 million and $13 million in matching funds for the project.

Veterans and historians filed lawsuits to block construction, arguing the project requires congressional authorization and would obstruct views between Arlington National Cemetery and the Lincoln Memorial.

The Commission of Fine Arts, stacked with Trump appointees, will review the designs next week, continuing efforts to reshape Washington architecture alongside the halted $400 million White House ballroom renovation.
title: Trump administration releases renderings of 250-foot 'triumphal arch' to loom over Potomac

bias: Left outlets frame the White House memo as evidence of institutional misconduct, using phrases like "quietly warned" and "mysteriously well‑timed bets" to stress suspicion and ethical breach, while right outlets amplify scandal and national‑security alarm—invoking " insider trading ," claims traders "made millions," and metaphors like "War Is a Casino"—to portray corruption and cover‑up.
Tactics differ: left leans on moralized language and implied secrecy; right uses escalation and profit figures to outrage.
Centers de‑emphasize guilt, stressing "no public evidence" and neutral verbs.
All sides agree a warning was sent after unusual trades on Kalshi/Polymarket, highlighting ideological divides over elite accountability versus scandal.
summary: The White House Management Office warned staff members on March 24 against using nonpublic information to place bets on prediction markets regarding Iran, noting that misuse of such information is a criminal offense.

Suspicious trading on oil futures occurred minutes before President Donald Trump announced a postponement of strikes on Iran's energy infrastructure on March 23, raising concerns about potential insider trading .

Earlier this week, New York Democrat Rep Ritchie Torres urged Securities and Exchange Commission Chair Paul Atkins and Commodity Futures Trading Commission Chair Michael Selig to investigate, calling the timing "a statistical impossibility."

On Friday, White House spokesman Davis Ingle dismissed reports of Administration officials insider trading as "baseless and irresponsible," stating federal employees are prohibited from using nonpublic information for private benefit.

Platforms like Kalshi and Polymarket recently tightened insider trading rules as scrutiny increases over prediction markets, which have increasingly attracted wagers on geopolitical events including potential nuclear conflicts.
title: White House says it warned staff against market bets amid Iran war
